title: Gambas al ajillo
subtitle: Spanish garlic prawns
excerpt: King prawns sizzled in olive oil with garlic, chilli, and a splash of dry sherry, served straight from the pan with bread to mop up the oil. The whole dish takes ten minutes and is one of the best things Spanish cooking has produced. Serves four as a starter.
sourceNotes: Gambas al ajillo is one of the canonical tapas dishes of Spain, found in virtually every bar from Madrid to Seville. The dish is documented by Claudia Roden in The Food of Spain (2011) and Penelope Casas in Tapas: The Little Dishes of Spain (1985). The use of dry sherry (fino or manzanilla) in the pan is the Andalusian version.

content.text: Gambas al ajillo is a dish that depends on heat and speed. The oil goes in very hot, the garlic turns golden in under a minute, the prawns go in and are done in two minutes. If you take longer than that, the prawns overcook and the garlic burns. The reward for moving quickly is one of the most fragrant, flavourful dishes in the Spanish canon.

The sherry is not essential but it is worth using. A splash of dry fino or manzanilla added after the prawns hit the pan cooks off in seconds and leaves a slight sweetness behind the garlic. The oil at the end of the dish is as important as the prawns — it should go into a bowl with plenty of bread.

items.prepNote: raw, peeled, tail on — pat dry before cooking
or one small dried red chilli, crumbled

content.text: A wide frying pan or a traditional terracotta cazuela (if you have one) is best. The prawns should fit in a single layer so they sear rather than steam. Serve with plenty of crusty white bread.

Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide frying pan over high heat until it shimmers. Add {{garlic}} of sliced garlic and {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes. Stir for 30 seconds to 1 minute until the garlic is pale golden — do not let it brown. Add the prawns in a single layer and season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Cook for 1 minute without moving them. Pour in {{sherry-dry}} of sherry if using and cook for 30 seconds more, then flip each prawn and cook for another 30 seconds to 1 minute until just pink through. Scatter over the parsley, squeeze over a little lemon, and serve immediately in the pan.

items.cause: Cooked too long. Prawns need only 2 minutes total in a hot pan.
items.fix: Watch for the colour change: as soon as the prawns are pink all the way through and curled into a loose C shape, they are done. A tight curl means overcooked.

items.cause: Pan not hot enough when garlic went in, so it cooked too slowly and darkened before the prawns were added.
items.fix: The oil must be very hot before the garlic goes in. Golden, not brown — if it darkens, discard and start again.

items.cause: Low-quality olive oil, or garlic and chilli not cooked long enough to flavour the oil.
items.fix: Use olive oil with some flavour to it. Let the garlic and chilli go for a full 45 seconds before the prawns go in.

content.text: Gambas al ajillo is found on the tapas menu of every bar in Spain, from the most basic neighbourhood place to the most formal restaurant. The dish changes slightly by region: in Madrid it is made in a small earthenware cazuela brought to the table still sizzling; in Andalusia the sherry is standard; on the coast the prawns are often freshly caught that morning. The formula is always the same: hot oil, garlic, prawns, speed. It is one of those dishes that looks simple and is simple, which makes it easy to do badly and, when done well, worth seeking out.
